1: “Then David said, ‘This is the house of the LORD God, and this is the altar of burnt offering for Israel.’” David’s declaration transforms the threshing floor into sacred space — the place where judgment was stayed, sacrifice was offered, and fire fell from heaven is designated the permanent dwelling of God. Jesus makes the same move about his body: “Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up” (John 2:19-21). As the threshing floor became the temple, the resurrection body of Christ is the true and final temple — and his body, the church, is now the “holy temple in the Lord” (Ephesians 2:21).
2: David commands the resident aliens (gēr) in Israel to be gathered — stonecutters to prepare building materials. The employment of non-Israelite workers in temple preparation echoes the Solomonic corvée (1 Kings 5:13-18) and foreshadows the nations’ contribution to the eschatological temple: “the wealth of the nations shall come to you” (Isaiah 60:5); “the kings of the earth will bring their glory into it” (Revelation 21:24). The Gentiles who contribute their skills to God’s house are a type of the nations streaming to Zion.
3: David prepares iron in abundance for the nails for the doors of the gates and for clamps, and bronze in quantities beyond weighing. The vast material preparation for a building David will never complete is itself a theological act: David pours himself into a project whose completion he will not see, trusting that God will bring it to fulfillment through his son. The disciples of Jesus are called to the same posture — working for a kingdom whose fullness they will not see in their lifetimes (Hebrews 11:13-16: they “died in faith, not having received the things promised”).
4: Cedar timber beyond counting, for Sidon and Tyre bring cedar logs without limit to David. The commerce of Phoenicia serving the temple of YHWH anticipates Isaiah’s vision of the nations bringing their wealth to the glory of Jerusalem (Isaiah 60:1-14) and Revelation’s vision of the nations’ kings bringing their glory into the New Jerusalem (Revelation 21:24-26). The universal provision of temple materials from all directions is a sign of the cosmic scope of God’s house.
5: “Solomon my son is young and inexperienced, and the house that is to be built for the LORD must be exceedingly magnificent.” David prepares abundantly precisely because the builder is young and the task exceeds human capacity. The Father’s provision of resources for his Son’s mission mirrors this: Jesus was sent into the world with the full provision of the Spirit (John 3:34: “he gives the Spirit without measure”) to accomplish the task that no mere man could complete. David’s vast preparation is the shadow of the Father’s lavish provision for the Son.
6: David calls Solomon his son and charges him to build the house of YHWH the God of Israel. The father-to-son transmission of the divine commission is the model of apostolic sending: as the Father sent the Son, the Son sends the apostles (John 20:21), and the apostles send their successors (2 Timothy 2:2). The Davidic transmission of the temple-building commission is the OT type of the Trinitarian sending that structures the entire mission of God.
7: “My son, I had it in my heart to build a house to the name of YHWH my God. But the word of YHWH came to me, saying, ‘You have shed much blood and have waged great wars. You shall not build a house to my name.’” David is a great king whose very greatness — the wars that secured the kingdom — disqualifies him from the work of peace. God does not reject David but redirects him: the warrior prepares; the man of peace builds. The Chronicler anticipates Paul’s theology of grace: God works through human limitation, not despite it.
8: “Behold, a son shall be born to you who shall be a man of rest. I will give him rest from all his surrounding enemies. For his name shall be Solomon, and I will give peace and quiet to Israel in his days.” Solomon (Šᵉlōmōh) derives from šālôm (peace) — the man of rest and peace who builds what the warrior king could not. Jesus is explicitly identified as our peace: “he himself is our peace, who has made us both one and has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility” (Ephesians 2:14). Solomon’s shalom-kingdom is the type; Christ’s kingdom of peace is the antitype — established not by military victory but by the cross, the ultimate shedding of blood that paradoxically ends the need for blood-shedding.
9: Solomon will be a man of rest, and YHWH gives him rest from all his enemies — this is why his name is Solomon (Šᵉlōmōh = peace). The rest YHWH gives to Solomon from his enemies is the typological template for the rest Christ provides: “Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest” (Matthew 11:28); “there remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God” (Hebrews 4:9). The peace-rest of the Solomonic era is the shadow of the eternal rest that the Son of David offers to all who come to him.
10: “He shall build a house for my name. He shall be my son, and I will be his father, and I will establish his royal throne in Israel forever.” The father-son formula and the eternal throne promise crystallize the Davidic covenant of 2 Sam 7. The author of Hebrews quotes this verse (combining 2 Samuel 7:14 and Psalms 2:7) as fulfilled in Christ: “To which of the angels did God ever say, ‘You are my Son, today I have begotten you’? Or again, ‘I will be to him a father, and he shall be to me a son’?” (Hebrews 1:5). The son who builds the Father’s house in 1 Chronicles 22 is the type whose ultimate fulfillment is the eternal Son who is himself the house of God (John 2:21; Ephesians 2:20-22).
11: “Now, my son, the LORD be with you, so that you may succeed in building the house of YHWH your God, as he has spoken concerning you.” The paternal blessing formula — YHWH be with you — is the identical formula with which the angel greets Mary (Luke 1:28) and which YHWH spoke to every figure at the threshold of a major commission (Joshua 1:5; Judges 6:12; 2 Chronicles 15:2). The divine presence-promise is the condition of all successful kingdom-building: “Unless the LORD builds the house, those who build it labor in vain” (Psalms 127:1).
12: “Only, may the LORD grant you discretion and understanding, that when he gives you charge over Israel you may keep the law of the LORD your God.” Wisdom for obedience — śēkel and bînāh (prudence and understanding) — is what David asks for Solomon. The same request underlies the NT’s theology of the Spirit as the Spirit of wisdom and revelation (Ephesians 1:17; Isaiah 11:2). Christ, in whom “are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge” (Colossians 2:3), is the source of the wisdom that makes obedience possible — the one in whom discretion and understanding find their perfect embodiment.
13: “Then you will prosper if you are careful to observe the statutes and the rules that the LORD commanded Moses for Israel. Be strong and courageous. Fear not; do not be dismayed.” The Deuteronomistic charge to Joshua (Joshua 1:6-9) is here repeated to Solomon: covenant obedience is the condition of prosperity and courage. Christ fulfills this command from the inside out — not as external law but as the living Torah (John 1:14; 1 Chronicles 14:6), who embodies the statutes and rules in his own person and empowers his people to walk in them (Romans 8:4; 2 Corinthians 3:3).
14: David has prepared 100,000 talents of gold, 1,000,000 talents of silver, and bronze and iron beyond weighing — timber and stone besides. The numbers are theological rather than historical: they signal incomprehensible abundance, the lavishness of divine provision. As the Father has prepared every spiritual blessing for the Son’s people in Christ (Ephesians 1:3) — riches that are “unsearchable” (Ephesians 3:8) — David’s material preparation is a material type of the inexhaustible spiritual provision of the new covenant.
15: “With you there are workmen in abundance: stonecutters, masons, carpenters, and all kinds of craftsmen without number, skilled in working gold, silver, bronze, and iron.” The gifted builders for the temple are a type of the Spirit-gifted community that builds the living temple: “to each is given the manifestation of the Spirit for the common good” (1 Corinthians 12:7; Ephesians 4:11-12). The temple-builders of David’s preparation represent every member of the body of Christ whose Spirit-given gifts contribute to the building up of the church.
16: “Arise and be doing! The LORD be with you!” David’s charge to Solomon — qûm wᵃʿăśēh (arise and act/do) — is the language of commission: the same word (qûm) used when the Spirit stirs Samson (Judges 13:25), when Joshua is charged to rise and cross the Jordan (Joshua 1:2), when the prophet is sent. Christ’s commission to his disciples is in the same register: “Go therefore and make disciples of all nations” (Matthew 28:19) — the imperative of mission entrusted to those whom the Lord equips.
17: David commands all the leaders of Israel to help Solomon his son, saying: YHWH your God is with you, and has given you rest on every side. The Davidic king enlists the entire leadership of Israel in the temple project — the community-wide mobilization of all resources for God’s house. Paul’s vision of the whole church building up the body of Christ (Ephesians 4:15-16; 1 Corinthians 3:9-15) echoes this total mobilization: no one is exempt from contributing to the building of the living temple.
18: “Is not the LORD your God with you? And has he not given you rest on every side? For he has delivered the inhabitants of the land into my hand, and the land is subdued before the LORD and before his people.” The rest from enemies is the condition for temple-building: the wars must end before the house of peace can be built. Christ’s declaration — “It is finished” (John 19:30) — is the cosmic shalom that makes permanent temple-building possible: the warfare between God and humanity is over, the enemies are subdued, and the people can now build in peace.
19: “Now set your mind and heart to seek the LORD your God. Arise and build the sanctuary of the LORD God, so that the ark of the covenant of the LORD and the holy vessels of God may be brought into a house built for the name of the LORD.” The charge ends where Chronicles always returns: dāraš YHWH (seek the LORD) is the heart-disposition from which all true action flows. Christ is the ultimate Seeker-of-the-Father and the one who enables his people to seek: “seek first the kingdom of God and his righteousness” (Matthew 6:33). The ark and the holy vessels that are to be housed — the presence of God dwelling among his people — find their final antitype in Christ, the one in whom “all the fullness of God was pleased to dwell” (Colossians 1:19; John 1:14).
